DEFENDANTS ERIK MAYO, TERRA INCOGNITA CAPITAL LLC'S Motion Designating Material Highly Confidential Pursuant To Protective Order

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr., Clerk: M. Goodman, Not Reported. (302/RBU)

Notice Of Motion And Motion Designating Material Highly Confidential Pursuant To Protective Order
Off Calendar Mar-22-2023 Continued To Apr-13-23 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 302
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: DEFENDANTS ERIK MAYO, and TERRA INCOGNITA CAPITAL LLC's Motion Designating Material Highly Confidential Pursuant To Protective Order.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

An August 2022 "protective order between plaintiff and defendants" governs information disclosure in this action. Under the order, parties may use the designation "highly confidential information - attorneys' eyes only." Plaintiff now seeks to "de-designate" certain AEO information so he "can be part of knowing the confidential evidence being discovered so he can make informed decisions about prosecuting his case." (Opp. 9:1-3.) Whether this is a proper de-designation ground under the protective order is not clear. Thus, each side is to file a five-page supplemental brief on that point in Department 302 by April 3, 2023. Hearing of the pending motion is continued to April 13, 2023 in Department 302.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Apr-14-2022 Continued To Jul-06-22 At 10:30 A.M. In Dept 610

MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-23-2022 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
No appearances. The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT ERIK MAYO, TERRA INCOGNITA CAPITAL LLC MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT

Defendants Erik Mayo and Terra Incognita Capital LLC's "special motion to strike Plaintiff's unverified complaint (CCP sec. 425.16)" is denied.

When evaluating an anti-SLAPP motion, this Court engages in a two-step analysis. First, "the moving defendant bears the burden of establishing that the challenged allegations or claims 'aris[e] from' protected activity in which the defendant has engaged." (Wilson v. Cable News Network, Inc. (2019) 7 Cal.5th 871, 884.) Defendant meets its prong one burden "by demonstrating that the act underlying the plaintiff's cause fits one of the categories spelled out in section 425.16, subdivision (e)." (City of Cotati v. Cashman (2002) 29 Cal.4th 69, 78.) Once the defendant meets prong one, the burden shifts to plaintiff to demonstrate a "probability of prevailing" on its causes of action (prong 2). (Rusheen v. Cohen (2006) 37 Cal.4th 1048, 1056.) In the second step, "the plaintiff must demonstrate its claims have at least minimal merit. If the plaintiff fails to meet that burden, the court will strike the claim." (Wilson, 7 Cal.5th 871 at 884.)

"Courts deciding an anti-SLAPP motion thus must consider the claim's elements, the actions alleged to establish those elements, and whether those actions are protected." (Bonni v. St. Joseph Health Sys. (2021) 11 Cal.5th 995, 1015.)

Order is signed.

Plaintiff's first and second causes of action are for violation of the Uniform Voidable Transactions Act ("UVTA") and "common law fraudulent transfer and aiding and abetting thereof." Under the UVTA, "[a] fraudulent conveyance is a transfer by the debtor of property to a third person undertaken with the intent to prevent a creditor from reaching that interest to satisfy its claim." (Lo v. Lee (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1065, 1071.) Thus, the acts underlying these causes of action, fraudulent transfers of funds, do not fit one of the categories spelled out in section 425.16, subdivision (e). Plaintiff's third cause of action for "common law tort of another" is derivative of the first two causes of action and the same conclusion follows.

The Court finds that allegations in paragraphs 10, 13, and 19 of the complaint are merely "collateral or incidental allusions to protected activity" which do not trigger application of the anti-SLAPP statute. (Oakland Bulk & Oversized Terminal, LLC v. City of Oakland (2020) 54 Cal.App.5th 738, 753.) Plaintiff's causes of action do not "arise from" these protected activities.

Defendants also argue that their conduct is protected under the litigation privilege set forth in Civil Code section 47. But that privilege "is not coextensive with the first prong of the anti-SLAPP statute. If the privilege is applicable, it would be considered under the second prong of the anti-SLAPP statute-likelihood of success on the merits-only if the activity was protected under the first prong of the anti-SLAPP statute. As we conclude that the activity is not protected, we do not need to reach the litigation privilege issue." (Chodos v. Cole (2012) 210 Cal.App.4th 692, 706 (citations omitted).) As the Court concludes that Plaintiff's causes of action do not arise from protected activities, it need not reach the litigation privilege issue.

Order is signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Clerk: W. Trupek, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano CSR# 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com mailto:maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. (302/RBU)
